There are **28** Epic issues in the SMP project. All of them are currently in the "To Do" status with "Medium" priority.

The SMP project contains 910 issues, primarily focused on motorcycle maintenance, quality audits, and software development for the Flet framework. Below is a summary of the issues categorized by their nature:

### 1. Quality Audits & Website Findings
*   **[SMP-912](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-912)** to **[SMP-905](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-905)**: A series of quality audits and findings for various websites (e.g., Acme Print, Vistaprint clones).

### 2. Motorcycle Maintenance & Mechanical Issues
A large portion of the project tracks mechanical defects across various motorcycle brands:
*   **Harley-Davidson**: Issues include cold master leaks (**[SMP-898](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-898)**), idle overheating (**[SMP-892](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-892)**), and parasitic battery drains on the Pan America model (**[SMP-504](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-504)**).
*   **BMW**: Frequent reports of driveshaft failures and fork stanchion separation on R1200GS models (**[SMP-464](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-464)**, **[SMP-455](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-455)**), and brake pressure loss on R1250GS (**[SMP-472](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-472)**).
*   **Yamaha**: Issues with cam chain noise and snatchy throttles on MT-09 models (**[SMP-729](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-729)**, **[SMP-328](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-328)**), and fuel tank bracket fractures on YZF-R3 (**[SMP-359](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-359)**).
*   **Ducati**: Overheating complaints in urban traffic for the Panigale V4 (**[SMP-489](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-489)**) and rear brake pressure loss on the Monster 821 (**[SMP-749](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-749)**).
*   **KTM**: Reports of radiator fan failures and instrument cluster water ingress on 390 Duke models (**[SMP-560](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-560)**, **[SMP-284](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-284)**).

### 3. Software Development (Flet Framework)
Issues related to the development, bug fixing, and feature enhancement of the Flet framework:
*   **Bugs**: Inconsistent padding in Cupertino buttons (**[SMP-106](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-106)**), TextField Enter key firing twice (**[SMP-83](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-83)**), and memory leaks in ListView (**[SMP-18](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-18)**).
*   **Features**: Adding global shortcut support (**[SMP-91](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-91)**), Map component integration (**[SMP-82](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-82)**), and Chart components (**[SMP-99](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-99)**).
*   **Tasks**: Setting up GitHub Actions (**[SMP-74](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-74)**) and upgrading the Flutter SDK to 3.19 (**[SMP-25](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-25)**).

### 4. General Fleet Maintenance
*   **[SMP-589](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-589)** to **[SMP-586](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-586)**: General maintenance tasks for Aprilia, Indian, Royal Enfield, and Suzuki fleets.
*   **[SMP-453](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-453)** to **[SMP-449](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-449)**: General maintenance for Honda, Kawasaki, Yamaha, Harley-Davidson, and BMW fleets.

### 5. Engineering & Campaigns
*   **[SMP-431](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-431)**: Triumph Tiger 1200 Shaft Drive Reinforcement campaign.
*   **[SMP-430](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-430)**: 2025 Ducati Panigale V4 Cooling System Upgrade.
*   **[SMP-441](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/SMP-441)**: CFD Simulation of airflow for cooling systems.

For a specific deep dive into any of these 910 issues, please let me know the issue key!

### **PLAT Project (Platform Engineering)**
The PLAT project is currently focused on three major initiatives: **Service Mesh Migration**, **Developer Portal Expansion**, and **FinOps Automation**.

#### **1. Service Mesh Migration (Istio 1.14 to 1.20)**

*   **[PLAT-14](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/PLAT-14)**: Implement zero-downtime sidecar upgrade automation.
*   **[PLAT-22](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/PLAT-22)**: Create Istio upgrade runbook and rollback procedures.

#### **2. Developer Portal & RBAC Modernization**
*   **[PLAT-26](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/PLAT-26)**: Developer Portal Golden Path Template Expansion.
*   **[PLAT-51](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/PLAT-51)**: RBAC System Modernization and Self-Service Enablement.
*   **[PLAT-56](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/PLAT-56)**: Implement RBAC API service with OPA policy engine.
*   **[PLAT-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/PLAT-60)**: Build Backstage plugin for RBAC self-service portal.

#### **3. Cloud Cost Optimization (FinOps)**
*   **[PLAT-76](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/PLAT-76)**: Cloud Cost Optimization and FinOps Automation Initiative.
*   **[PLAT-77](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/PLAT-77)**: Deploy Kubecost for granular Kubernetes cost allocation.
*   **[PLAT-81](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/PLAT-81)**: Implement automated resource rightsizing recommendations.
*   **[PLAT-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/PLAT-93)**: Implement idle resource detection and cleanup automation.

Based on an analysis of the API-related issues in the **BUGS** project, the common root causes and recurring themes are as follows:

### 1. Inconsistent Timezone and Datetime Handling
The most prevalent issue involves how the API processes and returns date/time information.
*   **Root Cause**: Lack of a unified serialization standard. Some endpoints return UTC with the 'Z' suffix, while others return server local time without any timezone indicator.
*   **Impact**: This causes ambiguity and bugs for integrations operating in different timezones.
*   **Related Issues**: 
    *   **[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97)**: Fix API returning inconsistent timezone handling.
    *   **[BUGS-98](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-98)**: Audit for datetime field consistency.
    *   **[BUGS-99](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-99)** & **[BUGS-100](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-100)**: Efforts to implement ISO 8601 UTC normalization and input parsing.

### 2. Rate Limiting Logic Errors (CORS/Preflight)
There is a specific logic flaw in how the API manages request quotas for web-based clients.
*   **Root Cause**: The rate limiter incorrectly counts **CORS OPTIONS preflight requests** toward the user's quota.
*   **Impact**: Single-page applications (SPAs) consume their rate limit twice as fast as expected (one for the preflight, one for the actual request), leading to premature 429 errors.
*   **Related Issue**: 
    *   **[BUGS-85](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-85)**: Fix rate limiter incorrectly counting OPTIONS preflight requests.

### 3. Lack of Standardization and Documentation
Several issues point to a broader "Developer Experience" (DX) gap where the API lacks consistent resource design and accessible documentation.
*   **Root Cause**: Fragmented API growth without a centralized design standard or interactive documentation portal.
*   **Related Issues**:
    *   **[BUGS-77](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-77)**: Design and implement consistent REST API v2 resource naming.
    *   **[BUGS-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-93)** & **[BUGS-94](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-94)**: Initiatives to set up OpenAPI (Swagger) documentation and a developer portal.

### 4. Cross-Tab Coordination Failures
In browser-based environments, the API interaction layer lacks synchronization between multiple open tabs.
*   **Root Cause**: Absence of a shared communication layer for token refreshing and state signaling.
*   **Related Issue**:
    *   **[BUGS-3](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-3)**: Implement BroadcastChannel API for cross-tab coordination.

Based on an analysis of the 100 issues in the **CRM** project, several systemic gaps in the customer support workflow are evident. The issues are largely focused on building infrastructure to solve these recurring manual bottlenecks:

### 1. High Support Volume from Account Recovery
A significant portion of the workflow is currently burdened by manual account recovery and identity verification.
*   **The Gap**: Lack of self-service options for locked accounts or lost credentials.
*   **Evidence**: Issues like **[CRM-26](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-26)** (Modernize Account Recovery Flow to Reduce Support Volume) and **[CRM-35](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-35)** (Automated Account Lockout Detection) indicate that agents are currently handling these high-frequency, low-complexity tasks manually.

### 2. Manual Billing & Refund Processing
The workflow appears to suffer from a lack of automation in financial disputes, leading to slow resolution times.
*   **The Gap**: Agents are manually retrieving transaction evidence and processing refunds across different payment gateways.
*   **Evidence**: **[CRM-2](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-2)** (Automated Transaction Evidence Retrieval) and **[CRM-51](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-51)** (Improve Refund Processing Efficiency) suggest that the current process is fragmented and requires manual data entry across Stripe, PayPal, and Braintree.

### 3. Technical Context "Blindness"
Support agents likely struggle to diagnose technical issues because they lack immediate access to the customer's environment or crash data.
*   **The Gap**: A "silo" between support tickets and engineering data (crashes/logs).
*   **Evidence**: **[CRM-76](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-76)** (Enhance App Crash and Technical Issue Reporting) and **[CRM-89](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-89)** (Customer Technical Environment Collection) show a push to automate the gathering of technical context that is currently missing from initial reports.

### 4. Reactive vs. Proactive Communication
The current workflow is reactive, meaning customers only get updates when they ask or when a human intervenes.
*   **The Gap**: No automated status tracking or proactive notification for known shipping/billing delays.
*   **Evidence**: **[CRM-56](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-56)** (Customer-Facing Refund Status Tracking Portal) and **[CRM-64](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-64)** (Proactive Refund Offers for Delayed Shipments) indicate that the current system relies on customers reaching out first.

### 5. Knowledge Management & Training Gaps
There is a pattern of inconsistency in how complex disputes or technical issues are handled.
*   **The Gap**: Lack of real-time guidance for agents and formal training modules for complex billing scenarios.
*   **Evidence**: **[CRM-18](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-18)** (Agent Training Module for Common Billing Disputes) and **[CRM-85](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-85)** (Automated Troubleshooting Guides) suggest that tribal knowledge is currently a bottleneck for scaling the support team.

### Summary of Systemic Needs:
*   **Self-Service**: Shift account and billing basics to customer portals.
*   **Integration**: Connect CRM directly to payment gateways and engineering logs (Sentry/PagerDuty).
*   **Automation**: Use ML/Rules engines for fraud detection and refund eligibility to remove human "middle-men" for standard cases.

There are currently 4 active issues in the **BUGS** project related to datetime and timezone handling.

### **Identified Issues**
1.  **[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97): Fix API returning inconsistent timezone handling**
    *   The API currently returns a mix of UTC with the 'Z' suffix, server local time without a timezone indicator, and ambiguous "date-only" fields. This causes integration failures for clients in different timezones.
2.  **[BUGS-98](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-98): Audit all API responses for datetime field consistency**
    *   A systematic review is needed to identify database columns storing local time instead of UTC and to categorize the risk level of existing inconsistencies.
3.  **[BUGS-99](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-99): Implement datetime serializer with ISO 8601 UTC output**
    *   The project needs a standardized JSON serializer to ensure all outgoing API datetimes use the ISO 8601 format with a `Z` suffix.
4.  **[BUGS-100](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-100): Add input datetime normalization**
    *   Incoming data is not being normalized correctly. The system needs to accept multiple formats (ISO 8601, Unix, etc.) but convert everything to UTC internally while logging warnings for non-UTC inputs.

---

### **Common Technical Root Cause**
The common root cause is a **lack of a unified datetime standard across the application layers**. Specifically:

*   **Inconsistent Serialization**: The application lacks a global serializer, leading to different endpoints formatting dates based on local server settings or default library behaviors.
*   **Ambiguous Storage/Retrieval**: Some database columns are likely storing "wall-clock time" (local time) without timezone offsets, making it impossible to determine the exact moment in time once the data is retrieved by a client in a different region.
*   **Missing Normalization on Ingress**: The system is "trusting" the input format of incoming API requests rather than forcing a conversion to a single internal standard (UTC) at the boundary.

Based on an analysis of the mobile-related issues in the **BUGS** project, the underlying technical problems causing user-facing symptoms can be categorized into four main areas:

### 1. Resource Management & Performance
Several issues stem from inefficient handling of device resources (CPU, Memory, Battery), leading to crashes and "jank."
*   **Memory Exhaustion (iOS):** **[BUGS-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-52)** causes crashes when viewing large attachments (>15MB) because the app attempts to load the entire file into memory rather than streaming or chunking it.
*   **Main Thread Blocking:** **[BUGS-72](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-72)** identifies that rich text rendering and synchronous image loading are happening on the main thread, causing frame rates to drop to 15-20fps during scrolling.
*   **Inefficient Background Processes:** **[BUGS-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-60)** reveals excessive battery drain (15-20% daily) due to background syncs running every 5 minutes, unnecessary location service requests, and unclosed WebSocket connections.

### 2. Network Resilience & Connectivity
The app lacks robust handling for real-world network conditions (3G, spotty WiFi, or offline environments).
*   **Silent Failures:** **[BUGS-56](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-56)** shows that the Android app "hangs" on poor connections because network timeouts (OkHttp) are not handled at the UI layer, leaving users with an infinite loading spinner.
*   **Lack of Offline Support:** **[BUGS-68](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-68)** highlights that users cannot create issues without an active connection, a critical gap for field engineers in low-connectivity areas.

### 3. Navigation & State Synchronization
Problems with how the app handles external triggers (notifications) and session state.
*   **Deep Link Race Conditions:** **[BUGS-64](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-64)** causes push notifications to open the wrong issue on Android. This is a technical timing issue where the deep link handler executes before the navigation stack is fully initialized.
*   **Token Refresh Race Conditions:** **[BUGS-2](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-2)** leads to intermittent logouts when multiple concurrent requests trigger a token refresh, invalidating the refresh token for subsequent requests.

### 4. OS-Specific Integration Failures
Issues arising from updates to native libraries or OS security requirements.
*   **Biometric API Incompatibility:** **[BUGS-18](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-18)** causes a crash during MFA enrollment on Android 12+ due to stricter `SecurityException` requirements in the updated AndroidX Biometric library.

### Summary of Technical Debt
| Symptom | Technical Root Cause | Issue Key |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **App Crashes** | Memory mismanagement (Large attachments) & Biometric API security exceptions | **[BUGS-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-52)**, **[BUGS-18](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-18)** |
| **Battery Drain** | Unoptimized background sync & legacy location code | **[BUGS-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-60)** |
| **UI Lag/Jank** | Main-thread markdown rendering & synchronous image loading | **[BUGS-72](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-72)** |
| **App "Hangs"** | Unhandled network timeout exceptions in the UI layer | **[BUGS-56](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-56)** |
| **Wrong Data Shown** | Deep link handler executing before navigation stack initialization | **[BUGS-64](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-64)** |

Based on the high-priority issues in the **CRM** project, the main security and fraud-related concerns center around account protection, identity verification, and refund fraud.

### **1. Fraud Detection & Prevention**
There is a significant focus on automating the detection of fraudulent activities, particularly regarding refunds:
*   **[CRM-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-60)**: Development of a **Refund Fraud Detection Model** using machine learning to identify suspicious patterns.
*   **[CRM-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-52)**: Implementation of an **Instant Refund Eligibility Engine**, likely designed to balance customer experience with risk controls for low-risk users.

### **2. Account Security & Access Control**
Protecting user accounts from unauthorized access and streamlining recovery are top priorities:
*   **[CRM-35](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-35)**: Development of **Automated Account Lockout Detection**, which is critical for mitigating brute-force or credential-stuffing attacks.
*   **[CRM-27](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-27)**: Implementation of **SMS-Based Account Recovery** to provide a secure alternative for users to regain access to their accounts.

### **3. Identity Verification**
To mitigate risks associated with high-value or suspicious transactions, the team is enhancing verification protocols:
*   **[CRM-31](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-31)**: Creation of an **Identity Verification Workflow** specifically for high-risk actions, ensuring an extra layer of security when it is most needed.

**Summary of Focus:**
The CRM project is currently prioritizing **proactive detection** (ML models and lockout triggers) and **strengthened authentication** (ID verification and secure recovery) to combat evolving fraud and security threats.

Based on the current open issues in the CRM project, there are several significant integration and alerting gaps that directly impact the speed of response to customer issues. These can be categorized into three main areas:

### 1. Lack of Automated Incident & Technical Alerting
Currently, the team lacks a direct link between technical failures and support visibility, leading to reactive rather than proactive support.
*   **[CRM-100](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-100)**: There is a pending need to create an alerting integration with PagerDuty for engineering.
*   **[CRM-95](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-95)**: Incident detection is not yet integrated with PagerDuty, which prevents support from knowing about outages before customers report them.
*   **[CRM-78](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-78)** & **[CRM-77](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-77)**: Sentry crash captures and in-app crash reports are not yet integrated with support tickets, meaning agents lack the technical context (logs/crashes) needed to resolve issues quickly.

### 2. Manual Data Retrieval & Siloed Information
Support agents are likely slowed down by having to manually check external systems for shipping and billing data.
*   **[CRM-82](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-82)**: There is no automatic bug ticket creation from CRM to Jira, requiring manual duplication of effort.
*   **[CRM-70](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-70)** & **[CRM-69](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-69)**: Integration with warehouse management and carrier APIs for return tracking is still in progress.
*   **[CRM-57](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-57)** & **[CRM-54](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-54)**: Refund status and payment provider APIs (Stripe/PayPal) are not fully integrated, forcing manual status checks.

### 3. Missing Proactive Customer Communication
The absence of automated status updates means customers must reach out to support for information that could be self-served.
*   **[CRM-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-93)**: The "Known Issues Status Page" with customer subscriptions is not yet built.
*   **[CRM-65](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-65)**: Shipping delay detection logic is not yet integrated with customer notifications.
*   **[CRM-14](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-14)**: Real-time chargeback alerts from payment providers are missing, delaying dispute resolution.

### Summary of Impact
The primary "gap" is a **lack of real-time synchronization** between technical health (Sentry/PagerDuty), logistics (Carrier APIs), and the CRM. This forces support staff to act as manual "information bridges," significantly increasing the time it takes to identify, investigate, and resolve customer complaints.

Based on the issue descriptions in the **BUGS** project, developer confusion regarding the API documentation stems from several core structural and content-related problems:

### 1. Static and Outdated Documentation
The primary source of frustration is that current documentation is maintained as a **static Markdown file** that is frequently out of sync with the actual API implementation.
*   **Impact**: Developers resort to "trial-and-error" or opening support tickets because they cannot trust the written docs (**[BUGS-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-93)**).
*   **Missing Features**: There is a lack of interactive "try it" functionality, multi-language code examples, and documentation for error scenarios (**[BUGS-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-93)**).

### 2. Inconsistent Resource Design (API v1)
The existing API (v1) suffers from inconsistent naming conventions and non-RESTful design, which makes it unpredictable for developers.
*   **Naming Conflicts**: Inconsistent pluralization (e.g., `/issue/{id}` vs. `/projects/{id}`) and mixed parameter styles (path params vs. query params) (**[BUGS-77](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-77)**).
*   **Error Handling**: Error responses vary wildly across endpoints, making it difficult for developers to implement consistent error-handling logic (**[BUGS-77](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-77)**).

### 3. Ambiguous Data Handling
Specific technical inconsistencies in how data is returned lead to integration bugs:
*   **Timezone Confusion**: Endpoints return dates in a mix of UTC, server time, or without any timezone indicator at all. This ambiguity causes significant issues for integrations operating in different timezones (**[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97)**).

### 4. Lack of Modern Developer Experience (DX)
The current API ecosystem is viewed as having "significant technical debt."
*   **Missing Capabilities**: Developers are hampered by a lack of modern features like webhooks and a proper developer portal (**[BUGS-76](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-76)**).
*   **Goal**: The team is currently planning an API v2 modernization initiative specifically to reduce API-related support tickets by 80% (**[BUGS-76](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-76)**).

### Summary of Issues
| Key | Summary | Root Cause of Confusion |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97)** | Inconsistent timezone handling | Mixed date formats (UTC vs. Server time) without indicators. |
| **[BUGS-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-93)** | Interactive API portal creation | Static Markdown docs are outdated; no "try it" feature. |
| **[BUGS-77](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-77)** | REST API v2 resource structure | Inconsistent naming (singular vs. plural) and varying error formats. |
| **[BUGS-76](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-76)** | API v2 Modernization | General technical debt and lack of webhooks/modern DX. |

Based on the CRM issue descriptions, there are several key monitoring and detection capabilities currently identified as missing or in the process of being implemented. These can be categorized into technical health, security/fraud, and business process monitoring:

### 1. Technical & Incident Detection
*   **Anomaly Detection & Baselines**: There is a missing capability for automated anomaly detection with established baselines to identify irregular system behavior (**[CRM-99](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-99)**).
*   **Proactive Pattern Recognition**: The system lacks automated "Technical Issue Pattern Detection" to identify recurring technical problems before they are manually reported (**[CRM-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-97)**).
*   **Environment Auto-Detection**: There is a need for a library to automatically detect customer web/mobile environments to assist in troubleshooting (**[CRM-90](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-90)**, **[CRM-89](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-89)**).
*   **Incident Integration**: Real-time integration between incident detection and alerting tools like PagerDuty is currently being addressed (**[CRM-95](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-95)**).

### 2. Security & Fraud Detection
*   **Fraudulent Recovery Attempts**: There is a gap in detecting and reducing fraudulent account recovery attempts (**[CRM-47](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-47)**).
*   **Security Analytics**: The security team currently lacks a "lockout analytics dashboard" to monitor account lockouts and potential brute-force patterns (**[CRM-38](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-38)**).
*   **High-Risk Identity Verification**: Automated workflows for flagging and verifying high-risk identity changes are missing (**[CRM-31](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-31)**).

### 3. Business & Financial Monitoring
*   **Chargeback Alerts**: Real-time alerts for chargebacks from payment processors are a missing capability being integrated (**[CRM-14](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-14)**, **[CRM-17](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-17)**).
*   **SLA Tracking**: There is a lack of automated tracking and reporting for Dispute Resolution SLAs (**[CRM-22](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-22)**).
*   **Proactive Refund Monitoring**: The system needs better visibility into delayed or problematic orders to trigger "Proactive Refund Offers" automatically (**[CRM-64](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-64)**).
*   **Auto-Refund Performance**: A monitoring dashboard for auto-refund rates and success metrics is currently required (**[CRM-55](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-55)**).

### 4. Customer Visibility
*   **Known Issues Status**: There is no automated "Known Issues" status page where customers can subscribe to updates on ongoing problems (**[CRM-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-93)**).
*   **Refund Tracking**: Customers currently lack a self-service portal to track the real-time status of their refunds (**[CRM-56](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-56)**).

Based on the current CRM subtasks, the team is focused on solving several key operational inefficiencies through automation, improved visibility, and system integration.

### 1. Manual Support & Incident Overhead
The team is working to reduce the manual effort required to manage support tickets and technical incidents:
*   **Automated Incident Detection**: Implementing anomaly detection (**[CRM-99](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-99)**) and ticket volume monitoring (**[CRM-98](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-98)**) to catch issues before they escalate.
*   **Duplicate Prevention**: Building ticket-to-incident linking (**[CRM-96](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-96)**) to prevent agents from working on redundant issues.
*   **Context Gathering**: Automating the collection of technical environment data (**[CRM-91](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-91)**) and user journey capture (**[CRM-80](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-80)**) so agents don't have to ask customers for basic details.

### 2. Fragmented Refund & Return Workflows
A significant portion of the work is dedicated to streamlining financial operations:
*   **Programmatic Returns**: Integrating carrier APIs for labels (**[CRM-69](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-69)**) and warehouse management systems (**[CRM-70](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-70)**) to trigger auto-refunds upon confirmation.
*   **Proactive Resolution**: Building logic to detect shipping delays and proactively offer resolutions (**[CRM-65](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-65)**, **[CRM-66](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-66)**) before the customer contacts support.
*   **Bulk Actions**: Implementing bulk refund capabilities with supervisor overrides (**[CRM-12](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-12)**) to handle mass issues efficiently.

### 3. Security & Account Recovery Friction
The team is addressing inefficiencies in how users recover accounts, which typically generates high ticket volume:
*   **Self-Service Recovery**: Building self-service unlock flows (**[CRM-37](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-37)**) and backup code systems (**[CRM-44](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-44)**) to reduce reliance on manual verification.
*   **Automated Risk Scoring**: Implementing account risk scoring (**[CRM-40](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-40)**) to automate the approval of low-risk recovery attempts while flagging high-risk ones for manual review.

### 4. Lack of Real-Time Performance Visibility
Operational "blind spots" are being addressed through:
*   **SLA Monitoring**: Implementing real-time SLA indicators for agent queues (**[CRM-24](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-24)**) and automated weekly compliance reporting (**[CRM-25](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-25)**).
*   **Dispute Management**: Automating evidence compilation for payment disputes (**[CRM-5](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-5)**) and building chargeback monitoring dashboards (**[CRM-17](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-17)**).

### 5. Knowledge & Training Gaps
To improve agent efficiency and decision consistency:
*   **Decision Support**: Building a "similar case search" (**[CRM-75](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-75)**) and an error pattern matching engine (**[CRM-87](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-87)**) to help agents find solutions faster.
*   **Internal Training**: Developing an integrated quiz and scenario presentation engine (**[CRM-19](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-19)**, **[CRM-20](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-20)**) to onboard agents more effectively.

Based on the design issues identified in the **BUGS** project, specifically within **[BUGS-77](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-77)**, the following inconsistencies in the REST API v2 design need to be addressed:

### 1. Naming Conventions and Pluralization
The current API (v1) suffers from mixed naming conventions that confuse developers. The v2 design must standardize these:
*   **Mixed Pluralization**: Currently, the API uses singular for some resources (e.g., `/issue/{id}`) and plural for others (e.g., `/projects/{id}`).
*   **Path vs. Query Parameters**: There is no standard for fetching specific records; some endpoints use path parameters while others rely on query parameters (e.g., `/getProject?id={id}`).

### 2. Endpoint Structure
*   **Non-RESTful Design**: Many v1 endpoints do not follow standard RESTful patterns. The goal for v2 is to move toward a clean, consistent structure, potentially following the **JSON:API** specification.
*   **Efficiency Issues**: As noted in **[BUGS-89](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-89)**, the current REST structure leads to "N+1 query patterns." For example, fetching an issue with its comments and attachments currently requires 4+ separate API calls.

### 3. Error Handling
*   **Varying Error Responses**: Error formats vary "wildly" across the current API. V2 needs a unified error response schema so developers can implement consistent error-handling logic.

### Summary of Required Fixes:
1.  **Standardize Resource Naming**: Adopt a consistent pluralization strategy (e.g., always use `/issues/` and `/projects/`).
2.  **Unify Parameter Usage**: Standardize on path parameters for resource IDs.
3.  **Standardize Error Schemas**: Implement a single, predictable JSON structure for all error responses.
4.  **Improve Data Fetching**: While GraphQL is being introduced as an alternative, the REST v2 design should aim to reduce the number of calls needed for related resources (e.g., through better resource nesting or inclusion parameters).

Based on an analysis of the CRM issues, the underlying causes of customer self-service gaps fall into four primary categories:

### 1. Lack of Real-Time Visibility
The most significant gap is the absence of status tracking for ongoing requests, forcing customers to contact support for updates.
*   **Refund Tracking**: Customers currently have no visibility into refund status, leading to "where is my refund" tickets that consume 15% of agent capacity (**[CRM-56](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-56)**).
*   **Dispute Status**: Billing disputes lack a structured tracking mechanism, resulting in resolution times averaging 7 days (**[CRM-1](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-1)**).

### 2. Rigid Account Security & Recovery Flows
Current security protocols are overly restrictive or lack modern recovery alternatives, accounting for 34% of all support tickets (**[CRM-26](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-26)**).
*   **Email Dependency**: Users who lose access to their email have no self-service path and must undergo a manual 2-3 day ID verification process (**[CRM-27](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-27)**).
*   **Generic Lockout Messaging**: Legitimate users who fail login attempts are met with generic error messages and forced to email support rather than being offered a secure self-unlock path (**[CRM-35](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-35)**, **[CRM-37](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-37)**).

### 3. Unstructured Intake Processes
The absence of guided self-service forms leads to inconsistent data collection and manual triage.
*   **Manual Dispute Initiation**: Users must use email or live chat to start billing disputes. This lack of a structured portal leads to inconsistent information capture and slower resolution (**[CRM-6](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-6)**).

### 4. Fragmented User Experience
Self-service options are often disconnected from the context where the user needs them.
*   **Accessibility Gaps**: Issues highlight the need to integrate "Report a problem" or "Track Refund" buttons directly into the **Order History** and **Confirmation Emails** to prevent users from defaulting to general support channels (**[CRM-6](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-6)**, **[CRM-56](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-56)**).

Based on an analysis of the rate-limiting issues in the **BUGS** project, there are three primary design flaws causing problems:

### 1. Incorrect Handling of CORS Preflight Requests
The most significant design flaw is that the rate-limiting middleware incorrectly counts `OPTIONS` preflight requests against the user's quota.
*   **The Problem**: Browser-based Single Page Applications (SPAs) trigger an `OPTIONS` request before most API calls. Currently, the system counts both the `OPTIONS` request and the actual request, effectively halving the user's available quota.
*   **Impact**: Users hit rate limits at 50% of their expected volume.
*   **Related Issues**: 
    *   **[BUGS-85](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-85)**: Fix rate limiter incorrectly counting OPTIONS preflight requests.
    *   **[BUGS-86](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-86)**: Exclude OPTIONS from rate limit middleware.

### 2. Lack of Client-Side Visibility (Missing Headers)
The current implementation lacks standardized feedback, making it impossible for developers to programmatically manage their request pacing.
*   **The Problem**: The system does not provide standard headers like `X-RateLimit-Remaining` or `X-RateLimit-Reset`. 
*   **Impact**: Developers cannot build "back-off" logic into their applications because they don't know how many requests they have left or when the window resets.
*   **Related Issues**:
    *   **[BUGS-87](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-87)**: Implement comprehensive rate limit headers.
    *   **[BUGS-88](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-88)**: Build user-facing rate limit monitoring dashboard.

### 3. Vulnerability to Distributed Attacks (IP-Only Scoping)
The rate-limiting logic relies too heavily on IP addresses, which is an outdated defense against modern attack patterns.
*   **The Problem**: Rate limiting is applied per IP address. Attackers are using residential proxy networks to distribute "credential stuffing" attacks across thousands of IPs, staying just under the 10 requests/minute threshold per IP.
*   **Impact**: A 400% increase in failed login attempts that bypass current limits. The design lacks behavioral analysis or account-based throttling to detect distributed patterns.
*   **Related Issue**:
    *   **[BUGS-22](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-22)**: Implement rate limiting bypass detection for credential stuffing attacks.

Based on the issues identified in the **BUGS** project, the root causes for mobile app battery and performance issues are categorized below:

### 🔋 Battery Drain Root Causes
The primary driver for battery issues is inefficient background activity and resource management, as detailed in **[BUGS-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-60)** and **[BUGS-16](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-16)**:

1.  **Inefficient Background Syncing**: The app runs sync cycles every 5 minutes regardless of user settings or whether new data is actually available.
2.  **Radio Wake-ups**: The sync process wakes the device radio unnecessarily, even when there is no data to transmit.
3.  **Persistent Connections**: WebSockets are being kept alive in the background, preventing the device from entering a low-power state.
4.  **Legacy Code Leaks**: Location services are being requested by legacy code even though the data is not used.
5.  **Polling Risks**: There is a recognized need to ensure that polling fallbacks (for devices without push capabilities) are paused when the app is in the background to prevent further drain.

### ⚡ Performance & UI Jank Root Causes
Performance issues, particularly "jank" (stuttering) during scrolling, are driven by main-thread congestion and poor asset handling in **[BUGS-72](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-72)**:

1.  **Main-Thread Rendering**: Rich text (Markdown) rendering is occurring on the main thread during scroll events, causing frame drops to 15-20fps.
2.  **Synchronous Loading**: Avatar images are loaded synchronously, which blocks the UI thread and interrupts smooth scrolling.
3.  **Expensive Calculations**: Cell heights for lists are calculated on the fly and are not cached, leading to high CPU usage during interaction.
4.  **Memory Management**: Inefficient cell reuse (holding 50+ cells in memory for a 100-item list) puts unnecessary strain on device resources.

### 📉 Stability and Compatibility
General performance is also impacted by high crash rates (up to 3.8% on Android), with specific root causes noted in:
*   **[BUGS-18](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-18)**: SecurityExceptions in the biometric MFA flow on Android 12+ due to strict crypto object initialization requirements in newer AndroidX libraries.
*   **[BUGS-51](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-51)**: A general lack of feature parity with the web dashboard and stability issues that have caused app store ratings to drop.

Based on an analysis of the high-priority issues and their associated initiatives in the **BUGS** project, several recurring technical debt patterns have emerged. These patterns indicate systemic issues in the codebase that are now manifesting as critical bugs or performance bottlenecks.

### 1. Concurrency and State Management Debt
A significant cluster of high-priority bugs stems from improper handling of asynchronous state and concurrent operations.
*   **Race Conditions**: **[BUGS-2](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-2)** (OAuth token refresh) and **[BUGS-3](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-3)** highlight a lack of cross-tab coordination and mutex handling in the authentication layer.
*   **Memory Leaks**: **[BUGS-6](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-6)** and **[BUGS-7](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-7)** point to "subscription debt," where auth state listeners are not properly cleaned up, leading to dashboard crashes.

### 2. Mobile Platform Fragmentation & Legacy APIs
The mobile applications (iOS/Android) are suffering from debt related to outdated handling of OS-specific behaviors.
*   **Version-Specific Crashes**: **[BUGS-18](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-18)** (Android 12+ MFA crashes) and **[BUGS-20](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-20)** show that the apps haven't been updated to handle modern biometric or permission APIs.
*   **Legacy Services**: **[BUGS-62](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-62)** explicitly mentions the need to remove "legacy location services and WebSocket backends" that are causing excessive battery drain (**[BUGS-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-60)**).

### 3. Data Consistency & Serialization Debt
There is a pattern of inconsistent data handling across the API and frontend, particularly regarding time and identity.
*   **Timezone Handling**: **[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97)**, **[BUGS-98](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-98)**, and **[BUGS-99](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-99)** reveal a lack of a unified datetime serialization strategy (ISO 8601), requiring a project-wide audit.
*   **Parsing Fragility**: **[BUGS-10](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-10)** (SAML parsing) and **[BUGS-43](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-43)** (Search syntax crashes) indicate that parsers were built without sufficient edge-case validation or iterative logic (**[BUGS-44](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-44)**).

### 4. Infrastructure & Scalability Debt (Elasticsearch)
The search infrastructure is currently a major source of "High" priority bugs due to version lag and inefficient indexing.
*   **Outdated Versions**: **[BUGS-27](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-27)** shows the system is still on Elasticsearch 6.8 (end-of-life), causing performance jank and relevance issues.
*   **Deep Pagination Issues**: **[BUGS-31](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-31)** and **[BUGS-33](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-33)** reveal that the search UI uses basic offsets instead of modern cursors (`search_after`), leading to crashes beyond page 10.

### 5. Error Handling & Resilience Debt
The system lacks a centralized strategy for handling failures, leading to "silent" bugs.
*   **Silent Failures**: **[BUGS-56](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-56)** and **[BUGS-57](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-57)** highlight that network requests fail without user feedback, necessitating a move toward centralized error-handling middleware and exponential backoff strategies.

### Summary of Impact
| Pattern | Primary Impact | Key Issues |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Concurrency** | Session/Auth instability | **[BUGS-2](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-2)**, **[BUGS-6](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-6)** |
| **Legacy Mobile** | Crashes & Battery Drain | **[BUGS-18](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-18)**, **[BUGS-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-60)** |
| **Serialization** | Data Inconsistency | **[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97)**, **[BUGS-10](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-10)** |
| **Search Debt** | Performance & Relevance | **[BUGS-27](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-27)**, **[BUGS-31](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-31)** |

Based on the CRM project issues, the current fraud detection gaps and the initiatives being developed to address them fall into three primary categories:

### 1. Refund and Billing Fraud
There is a significant focus on gaps within the refund process, where manual oversight is currently insufficient to catch sophisticated patterns.
*   **Lack of Automated Models**: **[CRM-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-60)** indicates a current gap in using Machine Learning to detect refund fraud, which is currently being addressed.
*   **Specialist Review Gaps**: **[CRM-63](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-63)** suggests that the system previously lacked a dedicated fraud specialist review queue with model explanations.
*   **Real-time Alerting**: **[CRM-14](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-14)** and **[CRM-8](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-8)** highlight a need for better integration of real-time chargeback alerts and automated dispute reason categorization to prevent billing abuse.

### 2. Account Security and Recovery
Several issues point to vulnerabilities in how the system handles account access and recovery, which are common targets for fraud.
*   **Account Takeover (ATO) Vulnerabilities**: **[CRM-47](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-47)** identifies a gap in reducing fraudulent account recovery attempts.
*   **Device Identification**: **[CRM-36](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-36)** shows a current lack of device fingerprinting to distinguish between known and unknown devices during login/recovery.
*   **Brute-Force Protection**: **[CRM-29](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-29)** addresses a gap in brute-force protection specifically for recovery code verification flows.
*   **Automated Lockout Detection**: **[CRM-35](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-35)** and **[CRM-38](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-38)** indicate that the system previously lacked automated detection for suspicious lockouts and the analytics to track them.

### 3. Proactive Detection and Monitoring
The system appears to have been reactive rather than proactive in identifying suspicious behavior.
*   **Anomaly Detection**: **[CRM-99](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-99)** and **[CRM-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-97)** are working to implement baseline anomaly detection and proactive pattern detection for technical and behavioral issues.
*   **Shipping and Logistics Fraud**: **[CRM-65](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-65)** identifies a gap in detecting shipping delays that could be exploited or indicate fraudulent logistics activity.

**Summary of Gaps:**
The primary gaps identified are a **lack of automated ML-driven fraud scoring**, **weak device-level authentication** during account recovery, and **delayed visibility into chargebacks and refund abuse**. The CRM team is currently addressing these by building specialized review queues, implementing device fingerprinting, and integrating real-time payment alerts.

Based on the mobile issues in the **BUGS** project, there are two primary network and error handling problems currently identified:

1.  **[BUGS-68](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-68): Lack of Offline Support for Issue Creation**
    *   **Problem:** The mobile app currently requires active network connectivity to create issues. Users in low-connectivity environments (warehouses, data centers) are forced to take manual notes and submit them later.
    *   **Requirement:** Implement a local sync queue that allows users to create issues and attach photos offline, which will then automatically sync once connectivity is restored.

2.  **[BUGS-17](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-17): Poor Session Expiry Error Handling**
    *   **Problem:** The app currently uses generic error handling for `401 Unauthorized` responses when a session expires or is revoked.
    *   **Requirement:** Replace the generic error with a user-friendly UI flow. This includes a specific modal explaining the security reason for the logout and offering biometric re-authentication to streamline the login process.

Based on the current issues in the **CRM** project, the team is addressing several key account security vulnerabilities and fraud risks. The work is primarily focused on hardening the account recovery process and implementing proactive threat detection.

### **1. Account Takeover (ATO) & Recovery Vulnerabilities**
A significant portion of the security effort is dedicated to securing the account recovery lifecycle, which is a common vector for unauthorized access:
*   **Fraudulent Recovery Attempts**: **[CRM-47](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-47)** specifically targets reducing fraudulent recovery attempts.
*   **Brute-Force Protection**: **[CRM-29](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-29)** is implementing brute-force protection for recovery code verification.
*   **Multi-Factor Recovery**: The team is diversifying recovery methods to move away from single points of failure, including SMS-based recovery (**[CRM-27](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-27)**) and backup codes (**[CRM-43](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-43)**, **[CRM-44](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-44)**).
*   **Identity Verification**: For high-risk changes, new identity verification workflows (**[CRM-31](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-31)**) and knowledge-based verification systems (**[CRM-41](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-41)**) are being built.

### **2. Fraud & Anomaly Detection**
The project is moving toward automated, data-driven security to catch malicious behavior in real-time:
*   **Anomaly Detection**: **[CRM-99](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-99)** and **[CRM-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-97)** involve implementing algorithms to detect baseline deviations and technical issue patterns.
*   **Risk Scoring**: **[CRM-40](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-40)** focuses on an account risk scoring algorithm to determine when additional verification is required.
*   **Refund Fraud**: **[CRM-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-60)** and **[CRM-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-52)** address financial vulnerabilities through machine learning models designed to detect refund fraud.

### **3. Visibility and Proactive Defense**
The team is also improving the tools available to both users and security admins to monitor for breaches:
*   **User Transparency**: **[CRM-34](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-34)** is building a user-facing security activity log so customers can monitor their own account for suspicious sessions.
*   **Automated Lockouts**: **[CRM-35](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-35)** and **[CRM-37](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-37)** automate the detection of suspicious activity and the subsequent account lockout/unlock flows.
*   **Security Analytics**: **[CRM-38](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-38)** provides a lockout analytics dashboard for the security team to track trends in account attacks.

### **Summary of Vulnerabilities Addressed:**

| **Unauthorized Access** | **[CRM-43](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-43)**, **[CRM-27](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-27)**, **[CRM-35](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-35)** |
| **Financial/Refund Fraud** | **[CRM-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-60)**, **[CRM-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-52)** |

Based on a review of escalation-related issues in the **CRM** project, the common causes of support ticket escalation problems fall into several key categories:

### 1. Lack of Formalized Engineering Escalation Paths
A primary driver of friction is the absence of a structured way for support to communicate with engineering.
*   **Inconsistent Communication**: Issues are currently logged in spreadsheets, sent via email, or mentioned in Slack, leading to them being lost or ignored (**[CRM-81](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-81)**).
*   **Missing Bidirectional Sync**: There is no automated way for support to see engineering updates or for engineering to easily access the original support ticket context (**[CRM-81](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-81)**).

### 2. Inconsistent Policy Application & Documentation
Escalations often occur because agents lack the authority or clear guidelines to handle edge cases.
*   **Refund Policy Exceptions**: Approximately 10% of refund requests (e.g., outside return windows or partial subscription use) don't fit standard policies. Agents escalate these inconsistently, and decisions often lack documentation (**[CRM-72](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-72)**).
*   **Account Recovery**: Inconsistent verification processes (e.g., some agents requiring ID photos while others require video calls) lead to varied outcomes and unnecessary escalations (**[CRM-39](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-39)**).

### 3. High Volume of Duplicate "Known Issue" Tickets
When widespread incidents occur (API outages, payment processor failures), the support team is flooded with duplicate tickets because there is no public-facing status page to deflect these inquiries (**[CRM-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-93)**).

### 4. Poor Data Quality for Engineering
When tickets *are* escalated, they often lack the structured data engineering needs to resolve them.
*   **Missing Environment Data**: Escalations frequently lack details like OS version or browser, which prevents engineering from reproducing bugs efficiently (**[CRM-92](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-92)**).

### 5. Visibility and SLA Tracking Issues
There is a general lack of monitoring for escalated issues, leading to "black holes" where tickets sit without progress.
*   **Missing Dashboards**: There is a need for centralized tracking of open escalations, time-in-status, and SLA compliance to prevent overdue issues (**[CRM-84](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-84)**, **[CRM-74](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-74)**).

### Summary of Key Issues:
| Cause | Impact | Related Issue |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Ad-hoc Escalation** | Bugs lost in Slack/Email | **[CRM-81](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-81)** |
| **Policy Ambiguity** | Inconsistent refund/recovery decisions | **[CRM-72](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-72)**, **[CRM-39](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-39)** |
| **Incident Noise** | Support flooded by known outages | **[CRM-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-93)** |
| **Poor Repro Steps** | Engineering cannot fix escalated bugs | **[CRM-92](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-92)** |
| **SLA Neglect** | Escalations sit idle without monitoring | **[CRM-84](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-84)** |

Based on an analysis of the 100 "To Do" issues in the **BUGS** project, several distinct technical patterns and recurring problems emerge. The backlog is currently structured around four major modernization and stability initiatives:

### 1. Authentication & Session Management (Reliability & Security)
A significant cluster of issues focuses on race conditions and state management within the auth flow:
*   **Concurrency Issues**: Race conditions in OAuth token refreshing (**[BUGS-2](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-2)**) and the need for mutexes to handle concurrent refreshes (**[BUGS-4](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-4)**).
*   **State Leaks**: Memory leaks in `useAuthState` hooks (**[BUGS-6](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-6)**) and a lack of cross-tab coordination for tokens (**[BUGS-3](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-3)**).
*   **Enterprise Integration Failures**: SAML assertion parsing is failing for enterprise customers (**[BUGS-10](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-10)**), requiring a more permissive attribute handler.
*   **Mobile Session Sync**: Session invalidations are not propagating to mobile devices (**[BUGS-14](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-14)**), leading to security gaps.

### 2. Search Infrastructure (Performance & Relevance)
The search system is undergoing a major migration and optimization phase:
*   **Version Obsolescence**: A critical need to migrate from Elasticsearch 6.8 to 8.x (**[BUGS-27](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-27)**).
*   **Pagination Failures**: Search results break beyond page 10 due to off-by-one errors and the lack of cursor-based (`search_after`) pagination (**[BUGS-31](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-31)**, **[BUGS-32](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-32)**).
*   **Parser Instability**: The search syntax parser crashes on complex boolean queries (**[BUGS-43](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-43)**), indicating a need for a more robust iterative parsing approach.
*   **Relevance Gaps**: Current scoring ignores issue severity and recency (**[BUGS-35](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-35)**).

### 3. Mobile Application Stability (Android & iOS)
Recurring technical debt in the mobile apps is causing crashes and performance degradation:
*   **Resource Exhaustion**: Large attachments are causing iOS crashes (**[BUGS-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-52)**), and background sync is causing excessive battery drain (**[BUGS-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-60)**).
*   **OS-Specific Regressions**: MFA enrollment is crashing specifically on Android 12+ (**[BUGS-18](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-18)**) due to biometric handling changes.
*   **Network Resilience**: The Android app fails silently on poor connections (**[BUGS-56](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-56)**), requiring a centralized error handling and retry strategy.
*   **Deep Linking Bugs**: Push notifications are opening the wrong issues on Android (**[BUGS-64](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-64)**).

### 4. API v2 Modernization (Consistency & DX)
The backend is struggling with technical inconsistencies across its endpoints:
*   **Datetime Inconsistency**: Inconsistent timezone handling and lack of ISO 8601 UTC standardization across API responses (**[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97)**, **[BUGS-99](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-99)**).
*   **Rate Limiting Flaws**: The rate limiter incorrectly counts `OPTIONS` preflight requests (**[BUGS-85](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-85)**), which can lead to false-positive blocking of legitimate traffic.
*   **Lack of Real-time Support**: A shift toward implementing Webhooks (**[BUGS-81](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-81)**) and GraphQL subscriptions (**[BUGS-92](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-92)**) to replace polling.

### Summary of Recurring Patterns:
1.  **Race Conditions** in state-heavy flows (Auth/Sync).
2.  **Memory Management** issues in both React (hooks) and Mobile (large attachments).
3.  **Legacy Infrastructure** (Elasticsearch 6.x) causing performance and feature bottlenecks.
4.  **Inconsistent Data Standards** (Timezones/Date formats) across the API surface.

Based on current open issues in the CRM project, there are several significant troubleshooting automation gaps that lead to manual overhead for agents and delays in resolution.

### 1. Manual Data Collection & Environment Detection
Agents currently spend significant time on "back-and-forth" messages to gather basic technical context.
*   **Gap**: Lack of automated environment capture (OS, App version, Network type).
*   **Impact**: Averages 2 extra messages per ticket just to get started.
*   **Planned Fixes**: 
    *   **[CRM-89](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-89)**: Standardized collection form for technical issues.
    *   **[CRM-90](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-90)**: Client-side library for auto-detecting web/mobile environments.

### 2. Manual Error Code Research
Users often report errors via screenshots or codes without context, requiring agents to manually look up resolution steps.
*   **Gap**: No automated mapping between error patterns and troubleshooting guides.
*   **Planned Fix**: **[CRM-85](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-85)** aims to auto-match error messages to a knowledge base of 50+ common errors and suggest guides to agents.

### 3. Disconnected Evidence Retrieval
For billing and transaction disputes, agents are manually pivoting between three different systems to gather logs.
*   **Gap**: Lack of a unified "evidence packet" for disputes.
*   **Impact**: 15–20 minutes of manual work per case.
*   **Planned Fix**: **[CRM-2](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-2)** will automate the retrieval of transaction logs, gateway responses, and IP geolocation into a single packet.

### 4. Reactive vs. Proactive Detection
The engineering team often learns about emerging technical issues from support ticket spikes rather than automated monitoring.
*   **Gap**: No real-time pattern detection for ticket volume anomalies.
*   **Planned Fix**: **[CRM-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-97)** to implement proactive engineering alerts when ticket volume for specific segments (e.g., a specific app version) exceeds 2x the baseline.

### 5. Incident Integration Gaps
There is a disconnect between customer-facing incidents and internal engineering alerts.
*   **Gap**: Manual escalation flow for support-detected issues and lack of auto-resolution sync.
*   **Planned Fix**: **[CRM-95](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-95)** to integrate incident detection with PagerDuty for automated creation and resolution of customer-facing incidents.

Across the **CRM** and **BUGS** projects, there are several requests to integrate with PagerDuty. These requests primarily focus on bridging the gap between customer support signals and engineering response.

### **Integration Points & Use Cases**

1.  **Proactive Technical Issue Detection**
    *   **[CRM-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-97)**: Aims to alert engineering via PagerDuty when ticket volume spikes (exceeding 2x baseline) for specific categories, app versions, or device types. The goal is to catch emerging issues that support sees before standard system monitoring detects them.
    *   **[CRM-100](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-100)**: Specifically requests PagerDuty alerts for "P1 patterns" where ticket volume is >5x the baseline in a critical category.

2.  **Incident Lifecycle Management**
    *   **[CRM-95](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-95)**: Focuses on bidirectional synchronization. It requests auto-creation of incidents from PagerDuty alerts tagged as "customer-facing" and ensures that when an incident is resolved in PagerDuty, it is automatically resolved in the CRM system.

3.  **Financial & Risk Monitoring**
    *   **[CRM-17](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-17)**: Requests a PagerDuty integration to alert the team if the rolling 30-day chargeback rate exceeds critical thresholds (0.4% for warnings, 0.5% for critical alerts).

4.  **Customer Communication (Indirect Integration)**
    *   **[CRM-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-93)**: While focused on a public status page, the requirement includes linking incident updates (which often originate from PagerDuty/Engineering) to customer subscriptions to reduce duplicate ticket volume during outages.

### **Summary of "Why"**
The primary drivers for these integrations are:
*   **Reducing Response Time**: Using support ticket volume as an early-warning system for engineering.
*   **Operational Efficiency**: Automating the creation and resolution of incidents to keep Support and Engineering in sync.
*   **Risk Mitigation**: Real-time alerting on financial anomalies like chargeback spikes.
*   **Deflecting Tickets**: Ensuring customers are notified of known incidents (triggered by PagerDuty alerts) before they file a new report.

Based on the descriptions in the **BUGS** project, several database, storage, and memory-related issues are implied, ranging from architectural scaling challenges to client-side resource exhaustion.

### 1. Database & Search Infrastructure
*   **Scaling Latency**: Search latency has degraded significantly (P95 at 4.2s) as the bug database grew to over 2 million issues. This has prompted an initiative to migrate to Elasticsearch 8.x for better performance and relevance **[BUGS-26](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-26)**.
*   **Schema & Migration**: There is a need to audit and migrate database columns storing local time to a consistent datetime format across all API responses **[BUGS-98](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-98)**.
*   **Vector Storage**: To support semantic search, the system needs to implement `dense_vector` fields in Elasticsearch, requiring an estimated 1.2GB of additional storage for 2.3M issues **[BUGS-49](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-49)**.

### 2. Caching & State Management
*   **Offline Storage**: The Android app requires a Room database implementation to cache issue lists and details for offline viewing **[BUGS-59](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-59)**.
*   **Distributed State**: Session revocation events are published to Redis but fail to propagate to mobile apps effectively, leading to security concerns where sessions remain active after password changes **[BUGS-14](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-14)**.
*   **Performance Caching**: Multiple issues highlight the need for caching layers to improve UI performance, including:
    *   Caching rendered Markdown content **[BUGS-73](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-73)**.
    *   Caching calculated cell heights for smoother scrolling **[BUGS-75](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-75)**.
    *   Caching embedding generation results to avoid redundant processing **[BUGS-48](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-48)**.

### 3. Memory Management & Resource Exhaustion
*   **Large Attachment Crashes**: The iOS app crashes (`EXC_RESOURCE`) when attempting to load attachments larger than 15MB because it loads the entire file into memory rather than using a streaming or preview approach **[BUGS-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-52)**.
*   **Memory Leaks**: 
    *   A significant leak exists in the dashboard's auth state subscription, where orphaned subscriptions consume memory and crash the browser tab **[BUGS-6](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-6)**.
    *   The parser requires a refactor to an iterative approach with a limited stack depth (10 entries) to prevent memory issues during complex parsing **[BUGS-44](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-44)**.
*   **Image Handling**: High memory usage is noted during image loading, leading to a requirement for downsampling and memory-limited streaming loaders **[BUGS-53](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-53)**, **[BUGS-74](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-74)**.

Based on an analysis of the 84 medium-priority issues in the **CRM** project, the team is addressing several critical incident management and operational gaps. The work is focused on moving from reactive support to proactive detection and structured resolution.

### 1. Detection & Alerting Gaps
The most significant gap being addressed is the lack of automated incident detection.
*   **[CRM-100](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-100)**: Integrating PagerDuty with engineering to ensure faster response times.
*   **[CRM-99](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-99)** & **[CRM-98](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-98)**: Implementing anomaly detection and ticket volume monitoring to identify outages before they are manually reported.
*   **[CRM-14](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-14)**: Adding real-time chargeback alerts to catch payment processing issues early.

### 2. Communication & Transparency Gaps
There is a clear push to improve how the company communicates during incidents.
*   **[CRM-94](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-94)** & **[CRM-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-93)**: Implementing a formal Status Page (Statuspage.io) and a "Known Issues" page with customer subscriptions to reduce duplicate support inquiries.
*   **[CRM-59](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-59)**: Automating status change notifications for customers.

### 3. Technical Context & Diagnostics Gaps
Support agents currently lack the technical data needed to resolve issues quickly.
*   **[CRM-91](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-91)** & **[CRM-90](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-90)**: Building auto-detection for technical environments (browser, OS, app version) to eliminate back-and-forth questioning.
*   **[CRM-78](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-78)** & **[CRM-80](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-80)**: Integrating Sentry crash reports and user journey captures directly into support tickets.

### 4. Workflow & Integration Gaps
The "wall" between Support and Engineering is being dismantled.
*   **[CRM-83](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-83)** & **[CRM-82](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-82)**: Creating bidirectional sync and automatic bug ticket creation between CRM and Jira.
*   **[CRM-96](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-96)**: Implementing ticket-to-incident linking to prevent duplicate work across teams.

### 5. Proactive Resolution Gaps
The team is moving toward "self-healing" or proactive support.
*   **[CRM-64](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-64)** & **[CRM-65](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-65)**: Detecting shipping delays and automatically offering refunds before the customer complains.
*   **[CRM-26](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-26)**: Modernizing account recovery flows to reduce the volume of manual security tickets.

### Summary of Impact
These initiatives suggest that the CRM team is transitioning from a **manual, reactive support model** to an **integrated, data-driven incident management framework**. By closing these gaps, they aim to reduce Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) and Mean Time to Resolve (MTTR) while simultaneously lowering ticket volume through better customer self-service and proactive communication.

Based on a review of 200 recent issues across the **BUGS** and **CRM** projects, the main categories of technical work being prioritized are:

### 1. **Security & Authentication Hardening**
A significant portion of work is dedicated to securing user accounts and stabilizing authentication flows.
*   **Account Recovery:** Modernizing recovery flows, including SMS-based recovery (**[CRM-27](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-27)**), backup codes (**[CRM-43](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-43)**), and identity verification via Jumio (**[CRM-33](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-33)**).
*   **Auth Reliability:** Fixing race conditions in OAuth token refreshes (**[BUGS-2](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-2)**) and resolving memory leaks in auth state subscriptions (**[BUGS-6](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-6)**).
*   **Threat Detection:** Implementing credential stuffing detection (**[BUGS-22](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-22)**) and account lockout automation (**[CRM-35](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-35)**).

### 2. **API Modernization (v2) & Developer Experience**
The **BUGS** project shows a major initiative (**[BUGS-76](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-76)**) to upgrade the API infrastructure.
*   **Standards:** Implementing OpenAPI 3.1 specs (**[BUGS-78](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-78)**) and consistent ISO 8601 datetime handling (**[BUGS-99](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-99)**).
*   **New Capabilities:** Adding GraphQL support (**[BUGS-89](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-89)**) and a robust webhook system for real-time events (**[BUGS-81](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-81)**).
*   **Governance:** Implementing query complexity limits (**[BUGS-91](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-91)**) and comprehensive rate limit monitoring (**[BUGS-88](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-88)**).

### 3. **Search Performance & Intelligence**
There is a concentrated effort to optimize search relevance and infrastructure.
*   **Infrastructure:** Migrating from Elasticsearch 6.8 to 8.x (**[BUGS-27](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-27)**) and implementing real-time indexing via Kafka/Debezium (**[BUGS-41](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-41)**).
*   **AI/ML:** Adding semantic search capabilities using vector embeddings (**[BUGS-47](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-47)**) and similar issue suggestion UI (**[BUGS-50](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-50)**).

### 4. **Billing & Refund Automation**
The **CRM** project is focused on streamlining financial workflows to reduce manual support overhead.
*   **Automation:** Building an instant refund eligibility engine (**[CRM-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-52)**) and automated transaction evidence retrieval for disputes (**[CRM-2](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-2)**).
*   **Fraud Prevention:** Developing machine learning models for refund fraud detection (**[CRM-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-60)**).
*   **Integrations:** Connecting with Stripe, PayPal, and Braintree for real-time chargeback alerts and automated processing (**[CRM-14](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-14)**).

### 5. **Mobile Stability & Offline Capabilities**
Improving the mobile experience is a key priority in the **BUGS** project.
*   **Performance:** Fixing scroll jank (**[BUGS-72](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-72)**) and excessive battery drain from background sync (**[BUGS-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-60)**).
*   **Offline Support:** Implementing local issue queues and sync logic for offline creation (**[BUGS-68](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-68)**).
*   **Crash Resilience:** Handling large attachments to prevent iOS crashes (**[BUGS-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-52)**).

Based on the current issues in the **BUGS** project, the primary rendering and performance bottlenecks affecting mobile users are centered around the issue list screen and general application stability.

### **Key Bottlenecks Identified**

1.  **Main Thread Blocking (Rich Text Rendering)**
    *   **[BUGS-72](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-72)**: Markdown and rich text rendering for issue descriptions (especially those with inline images or code blocks) are occurring on the main thread during scrolling. This causes frame rates to drop to **15-20fps** on mid-range devices.

2.  **Synchronous Asset Loading**
    *   **[BUGS-72](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-72)**: User avatars are being loaded synchronously, which blocks the UI thread and contributes to significant "scroll jank."

3.  **Expensive Layout Calculations**
    *   **[BUGS-72](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-72)**: Cell heights for the issue list are calculated dynamically without being cached, leading to redundant processing during scroll events.

4.  **Memory Management & View Recycling**
    *   **[BUGS-72](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-72)**: The application is holding too many cells in memory (50+ cells for a 100-item list), indicating inefficient view reuse logic.

5.  **General Stability & Crash Rates**
    *   **[BUGS-51](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-51)**: There is a broader performance initiative to address high crash rates (**2.3% on iOS** and **3.8% on Android**). Users have reported that the mobile experience is significantly slower and less stable compared to the web dashboard.

### **Summary of Affected Issues**
1. **[BUGS-72](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-72)**: Fix issue list scroll performance jank on both mobile platforms.
2. **[BUGS-51](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-51)**: Mobile Application Stability and Performance Initiative.
